courses.subject: LAHS
courses.title: Introduction to Liberal Arts and Human Sciences
courses.description: Introduction to the College of Liberal Arts and Human Sciences: discover majors, minors, and degree requirements in the College of Liberal Arts and Human Sciences. Introduction to academic skills and career options. Review of policies and procedures for academic planning and success. Review of University programs and services that support students and promote student development.

courses.subject: LAHS
courses.title: Academic Recovery and Success Strategies
courses.description: Helps students on academic probation and/or returning from academic suspension to develop academic skills, behaviors and motivation towards success; focuses on the development and application of college-level study skills, personal success strategies, and the use of campus resources that enhance individual student achievement. Credit not applicable to meeting degree requirements.

courses.subject: LAHS
courses.title: Introduction to Hip Hop Studies
courses.description: Interdisciplinary survey and examination of Hip Hop aesthetic and cultural expressions, history, and scholarship and their contexts in the U.S. and globally; including how Hip Hop comments on, responds to and reflects, and mediates intersectional identities, space, place, traditions, and histories of inequity and power in the U.S. and globally. Critical analysis and creation of multimedia texts in response to and synthesizing diverse perspectives in Hip Hop. Opportunities to engage and participate in a variety of campus and community organizations and resources.

courses.subject: LAHS
courses.title: Topics in Sports Culture
courses.description: Examination of fundamental concepts related to sports culture—including community, competition and excellence, rituals and traditions, exclusion and inclusion, commercialization and entertainment, ethics and integrity, risk and injury, cultural symbolism and meaning—in variable cultural, historical, political, and social contexts. Emphasis on the interpretation and analysis of texts and media across disciplines to understand sports culture and the social and cultural phenomena shaping sports from the playground to scale. Course may be repeated one time with different content for a maximum of 6 credits.

courses.subject: LAHS
courses.title: Introduction to Displacement Studies
courses.description: Examines key concepts, ideas, and technologies in global population displacement, including categorization, distribution and governance of displaced groups. Introduces displacement drivers such as natural disaster, climate change, civil unrest, infectious disease, and forced relocation. Identifies digital infrastructures used for, by, and against displaced populations. Describes experiences of displaced people.

courses.subject: LAHS
courses.title: Introduction to Public Interest Technology
courses.description: Introduces concepts, skills, and problems related to Public Interest Technology, an emerging field of comprehensive approaches to ensure that technology—broadly conceived to encompass digital, genomic, agricultural, financial, and other sectors—works for the benefit of public interest. Focuses on introducing basic concepts and frameworks in Public Interest Technology, including social justice, inclusive design, the politics of design and production, technology policy, and stakeholder identification and participation.

courses.subject: LAHS
courses.title: Communicating and Engaging with Science Capstone
courses.description: Capstone experience in in communicating and engaging with science. Transdisciplinary collaboration cooperating with peers in STEM and non-STEM fields. Engagement—via digital, performative, written, and visual approaches—with audiences with diverse levels of scientific understanding. Discussion of methods for assessing the impact of science communication. Senior standing required.

courses.subject: LAHS
courses.title: Transdisciplinary Enabling Capability Studio
courses.description: Experiential learning studio course. Immerses students in teams working with stakeholders to identify a sociotechnical problem regarding a transdisciplinary solution for which the stakeholder group has less access, awareness, adoption, or benefit compared to the broader population; conduct humanistic and social scientific research to better understand the problem; evaluate different policy and design solutions to the problem and create a concrete prototype of a solution to the identified problem. Students work with an external stakeholder partner (e.g., business, nonprofit organization, community group, government, or faculty mentor/team) to identify a pressing problem experienced by the partner or its users, customers, or constituents. Students first apply humanistic or social/behavioral scientific research methods (e.g., historical documentary analysis and focus group interviews) to better understand the nature and causes of the problem. Students evaluate potential solutions to the problem in light of what they found through their research and the potential for increasing fairness in decisions involving technology. Then, student teams prioritize design and prototyping process, working through difficulties and constraints any potential solution faces, and assessing strengths and weaknesses of proposed solutions in dialogue with their stakeholders. Students prototype potential solutions, whether a technological or process design or proposed policy or legislation, and have their prototype assessed, tested, and critiqued by both the instructor and representatives from the stakeholder group. Finally, students evaluate applications of their project and skills used to one or more future career settings.

courses.subject: LAHS
courses.title: Tech for Humanity Enabling Capability Studio I
courses.description: Two-semester experiential learning course sequence, which serves as experiential learning studio course, which serves as culminating capstone experience for the planned Tech for Humanity minor. Immerses students in teams working with stakeholders to identify a sociotechnical problem regarding a technology for which the stakeholder group has less access, awareness, adoption, or benefit compared to the broader population; conduct humanistic and social scientific research to better understand the problem; evaluate different policy and design solutions to the problem and how they contribute to social justice, including enhancing diversity, equity, access, and inclusion; and create a concrete prototype of a solution to the identified problem. LAHS 4115 In the first semester of the two-course sequence, students work with an external stakeholder partner (e.g., business, nonprofit organization, community group, or government) to identify a pressing sociotechnical problem experienced by the partner or its users, customers, or constituents. Students apply humanistic or social/behavioral scientific research methods (e.g., historical documentary analysis and focus group interviews) to better understand the nature and causes of the problem. Students evaluate potential solutions to the problem in light of what they found through their research and the potential for increasing social justice. LAHS 4116 In the second semester, student teams prioritize design and prototyping process, working through difficulties and constraints any potential solution faces, and assessing strengths and weaknesses of proposed solutions in dialogue with their stakeholders. Students prototype potential solutions, whether a technological or process design or proposed policy or legislation, and have their prototype assessed, tested, and critiqued by both the instructor and representatives from the stakeholder group.

courses.title: Tech for Humanity Enabling Capability Studio
courses.description: Two-semester experiential learning course sequence, which serves as experiential learning studio course, which serves as culminating capstone experience for the planned Tech for Humanity minor. courses.subject: LAHS
courses.title: Experiential Learning for Social Impact Capstone
courses.description: Capstone in displacement studies. Promotes social justice through community engaged research. Work with a research or cultural center on campus or a community organization off campus. Academy of Transdisciplinary Studies advisor approval. Pre: Junior Standing.
